preamble: The transformation from a voiceless uvular plosive /q/ to a voiceless alveolar plosive /t/ is a type of sound change known as fronting. This process involves the articulation of a sound moving forward in the vocal tract, from the uvular region to the alveolar ridge. It is a common phenomenon observed in phonological development, particularly in child language acquisition, where children simplify more complex back consonants to easier-to-produce front consonants.
phoneticEffects: Fronting, change in place of articulation (uvular to alveolar)

examples.note: In child speech, the uvular stop /q/ is fronted to an alveolar stop /t/. In this specific example, it becomes an emphatic alveolar stop /ṭ/ due to co-occurring emphasis in the word 'Day of Judgment'.

sources: Al-Tamimi, A. A., & Al-Harbi, S. S. (2024). Phonological substitution patterns in Yemeni Ibbi Arabic child speech: a markedness and natural phonology perspective. Journal of Psycholinguistic Research.
